title: Personal AI only works if it stays personal
summary: A personal essay about why personal AI only works when it resists flattening the person it claims to help.
topic: personal AI once maintenance became the real product
body: I wrote this down in April 2025 after thinking about personal AI from the vantage point of a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling. I kept drifting past the headline toward the quieter question underneath it: what kind of world was this idea written for? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. The conversation tends to favor capability over context, performance over maintenance, and scale over the simple question of livability. Personal AI seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The public version kept pointing at one mechanism, but it was another one that felt socially decisive to me. When people talk in public they reach first for the visible layer, the thing that demos cleanly and fits in a headline. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They quietly decide who may stay general and who must become machine-readable. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, personal AI stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. Because it resists tidy measurement, that difference tends to vanish from the official accounting. But mood is one of the outputs. If the system keeps turning ordinary life into a permission ladder, I assume that outcome belongs to the design. It is part of what the product is. Performance matters, but I have never trusted the argument that treats lived texture as an indulgence.

Looking back from April 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “On interface trust after the magic show,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. I found myself looking past the applause toward the maintenance burden, the politics, and the quieter emotional contract being written underneath the feature set. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was incomplete in the familiar way optimism becomes incomplete before the machinery has touched enough real lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can like a tool and still ask what sort of world it is slowly arranging around itself. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The sane version usually starts by distrusting the oversized story until the smaller one has earned the right to expand. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “I keep coming back to synthetic voice.”

A final note to myself from April 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. These days I care less about winning the prediction game than about noticing what a tool asks of the people standing closest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: Interface trust is built out of small legible things
I keep coming back to synthetic voice
